params.text:
Controlled drugs and PRN medicines

Controlled drugs (CDs) are medicines with extra legal restrictions under the Misuse of Drugs Act because of their risk of misuse — for example strong painkillers such as morphine. They carry additional requirements:

  • Stored in a dedicated, secured CD cabinet.
  • Recorded in a separate controlled drugs register, and in care homes a second trained member of staff usually witnesses and countersigns.
  • Running balances (stock counts) are kept and checked.

PRN — "when required" medicines

PRN means "pro re nata", or when required. These are given only when needed, such as pain relief. Each PRN medicine should have a clear protocol saying when to offer it, the maximum dose in 24 hours, and how to tell whether the person needs it — especially if they cannot ask. Always record what was given, why, and how much stock remains.

params.alt: Illustration for Controlled drugs and PRN

params.question: In a care home, controlled drugs are usually witnessed and countersigned by a second trained member of staff and recorded in a separate controlled drugs register.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Incorrect. Controlled drugs do carry extra requirements — a separate register, secure CD cabinet, and usually a second-staff witness in care homes.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. The extra witnessing, countersigning and separate register are additional safeguards required for controlled drugs.
